title: Citrus fruit and vegetable wash spray
subtitle: A diluted acidic spray with citric acid, white vinegar, and lemon essential oil for washing produce
excerpt: A slightly acidic spray made from diluted white vinegar, citric acid, and water helps loosen surface residues from fruit and vegetables before rinsing. Lemon essential oil adds fragrance. Spray onto produce, leave for a minute, and rinse well under cold running water. Not a substitute for rinsing leafy greens in water; they are too delicate for an acidic spray.
sourceNotes: Acidic produce washes: acetic acid (white vinegar) and citric acid lower the pH of the wash solution, which helps break down the waxy cuticle on some fruits and assists in loosening surface residues. Reference: Cook's Illustrated tested produce washes (2010) found diluted vinegar outperformed plain water for surface bacteria reduction. This recipe does not make any pesticide-removal claim beyond improved surface rinsing. Lemon EO: fragrance only at this dilution; completely safe for produce when the spray is rinsed away. Not suitable for leafy greens, which bruise in acidic solutions.

recipe.yieldDescription: Approximately 235 ml in a spray bottle
recipe.batchNotes: Double or triple the recipe and store in a larger bottle. Keeps well between uses.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ready to use immediately. Store in the spray bottle; no refrigeration needed.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Vinegar-based sprays are self-preserving; the acidity prevents microbial growth. Keeps 12 months at room temperature. Discard if the spray develops an unusual appearance or smell.

glossaryTerms.definition: The organic acid that gives white vinegar its sharp smell and acidic properties. At 5% concentration (standard white vinegar), acetic acid lowers the pH of a wash solution, which helps loosen waxy surface coatings and assists in surface cleansing of produce.

content.text: solution; at 5% concentration it lowers the pH of a wash water and assists in loosening the waxy surface layer on fruit such as apples and pears. Citric acid reinforces the acidic effect. The spray is meant to be applied, left briefly, and rinsed off thoroughly under cold running water; the residues it loosens wash away with the spray. Lemon essential oil adds fragrance only and is completely safe for produce when rinsed away. Do not use this spray on leafy greens such as spinach, lettuce, or herbs; they bruise and wilt in an acidic solution. For leafy greens, a soak in a bowl of plain cold water is better.

items.prepNote: 5% acetic acid; do not use malt or wine vinegar
Dissolve in the water before adding the vinegar
Fragrance only; safe to use on produce when rinsed away

content.text: Weigh the distilled water into the spray bottle. Add the citric acid and shake or stir gently until dissolved. Add the white vinegar and the lemon essential oil. Seal and shake briefly to combine. Label with the batch date.

Spray the wash evenly over the surface of firm fruit or vegetables: apples, pears, cucumbers, peppers, tomatoes, and similar produce. Leave for 1 minute. Rinse thoroughly under cold running water, rubbing the surface with your hands. Do not use on leafy greens or herbs. Always rinse completely before eating.

Store at room temperature in the spray bottle. The acidity of the vinegar and citric acid preserves the spray; it keeps 12 months. Shake before use. Discard if the spray develops an unusual smell or appearance.
